html:
AI ethics draws on four foundational principles from bioethics, adapted to the context of automated decision-making:

  • Autonomy: people should be able to understand and contest AI decisions that affect them.
  • Beneficence: AI systems should be designed to benefit users and society.
  • Non-maleficence: avoiding harm is not the same as doing good — both must be designed for explicitly.
  • Justice: benefits and risks of AI should be distributed fairly across groups.

These principles are widely cited but their application is contested. In practice, they often conflict: a system that maximises beneficence for the majority may violate justice for a minority.

Discussion prompt: Which principle do you think is most frequently violated in current AI systems, and why?
